title: Spherical coordinate system

infobox.value: specifies a given point in [three-dimensional space](/wiki/Three-dimensional_space "Three-dimensional space") by using a distance and two angles as its three [coordinates](/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system"). These are
**r** along the line connecting the point to a fixed point called the [origin](/wiki/Origin_(mathematics) "Origin (mathematics)");
**θ** between this radial line and a given *polar axis*;[[a]](#cite_note-1) and
**φ**, which is the [angle of rotation](/wiki/Angle_of_rotation "Angle of rotation") of the radial line around the polar axis.[[b]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Coordinates comprising a distance and two angles

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/3D_Spherical.svg/250px-3D_Spherical.svg.png)](/wiki/File:3D_Spherical.svg)

The ***physics convention***. Spherical coordinates (**r**, **θ**, **φ**) as commonly used: ([ISO](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") [80000-2:2019](/wiki/ISO/IEC_80000 "ISO/IEC 80000")): radial distance r ([slant distance](/wiki/Slant_distance "Slant distance") to origin), polar angle θ ([theta](/wiki/Theta "Theta")) (angle with respect to positive polar axis), and azimuthal angle φ ([phi](/wiki/Phi "Phi")) (angle of rotation from the initial meridian plane). ***This is the convention followed in this article.***

In [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), a **spherical coordinate system** specifies a given point in [three-dimensional space](/wiki/Three-dimensional_space "Three-dimensional space") by using a distance and two angles as its three [coordinates](/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system"). These are

* the **radial distance** **r** along the line connecting the point to a fixed point called the [origin](/wiki/Origin_(mathematics) "Origin (mathematics)");
* the **polar angle** **θ** between this radial line and a given *polar axis*;[[a]](#cite_note-1) and
* the **azimuthal angle** **φ**, which is the [angle of rotation](/wiki/Angle_of_rotation "Angle of rotation") of the radial line around the polar axis.[[b]](#cite_note-2)

(See graphic regarding the "physics convention".)

Once the radius is fixed, the three coordinates (*r*, *θ*, *φ*), known as a 3-[tuple](/wiki/Tuple "Tuple"), provide a coordinate system on a [sphere](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere"), typically called the **spherical polar coordinates**.
The [plane](/wiki/Plane_(geometry) "Plane (geometry)") passing through the origin and [perpendicular](/wiki/Perpendicular "Perpendicular") to the polar axis (where the polar angle is a [right angle](/wiki/Right_angle "Right angle")) is called the ***reference plane*** (sometimes *[fundamental plane](/wiki/Fundamental_plane_(spherical_coordinates) "Fundamental plane (spherical coordinates)")*).

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The physics convention is followed in this article; (See both graphics re "physics convention" and re "mathematics convention".)

The radial distance from the fixed point of origin is also called the *radius*, or *radial line*, or *radial coordinate*. The polar angle may be called *[inclination angle](/wiki/Inclination_angle "Inclination angle")*, *[zenith angle](/wiki/Zenith_angle "Zenith angle")*, *[normal angle](/wiki/Normal_vector "Normal vector")*, or the *[colatitude](/wiki/Colatitude "Colatitude")*. The user may choose to replace the inclination angle by its [complement](/wiki/Angular_complement "Angular complement"), the *[elevation angle](/wiki/Elevation_angle "Elevation angle")* (or *[altitude angle](/wiki/Altitude_angle "Altitude angle")*), measured upward between the reference plane and the radial line—i.e., from the reference plane upward (towards to the positive z-axis) to the radial line. The *depression angle* is the negative of the elevation angle. *(See graphic re the "physics convention"—not "mathematics convention".)*

Both the use of symbols and the naming order of tuple coordinates differ among the several sources and disciplines. This article will use the ISO convention[[1]](#cite_note-3) frequently encountered in *physics*, where the naming tuple gives the order as: radial distance, polar angle, azimuthal angle, or *![{\displaystyle (r,\theta ,\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d0fbf2e667b8c94c169165fc44e0d73e66c901a9)*. (See graphic re the "physics convention".) In contrast, the conventions in many mathematics books and texts give the naming order differently as: radial distance, "azimuthal angle", "polar angle", and ![{\displaystyle (\rho ,\theta ,\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0ea69ccecb586b659206091cb824b2bbf51b5498) or ![{\displaystyle (r,\theta ,\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d0fbf2e667b8c94c169165fc44e0d73e66c901a9)—which switches the uses and meanings of symbols θ and φ. Other conventions may also be used, such as *r* for a radius from the *z-*axis that is not from the point of origin. Particular care must be taken to check the meaning of the symbols.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/3D_Spherical_2.svg/250px-3D_Spherical_2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:3D_Spherical_2.svg)

The ***mathematics convention***. Spherical coordinates (*r*, *θ*, *φ*) as typically used: radial distance r, azimuthal angle θ, and polar angle φ. + *The meanings of θ and φ have been swapped*—compared to the **physics convention**. The 'south'-direction x-axis is depicted but the 'north'-direction x-axis is not. (As in physics, ρ ([rho](/wiki/Rho "Rho")) is often used instead of r to avoid confusion with the value r in cylindrical and 2D polar coordinates.)

According to the conventions of [geographical coordinate systems](/wiki/Geographical_coordinate_system "Geographical coordinate system"), positions are measured by latitude, longitude, and height (altitude). There are a number of [celestial coordinate systems](/wiki/Celestial_coordinate_system "Celestial coordinate system") based on different [fundamental planes](/wiki/Fundamental_plane_(spherical_coordinates) "Fundamental plane (spherical coordinates)") and with different terms for the various coordinates. The spherical coordinate systems used in mathematics normally use [radians](/wiki/Radian "Radian") rather than [degrees](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)"); (note 90 degrees equals π⁄2 radians). And these systems of the *mathematics convention* may measure the azimuthal angle *counterclockwise* (i.e., from the south direction x-axis, or 180°, towards the east direction y-axis, or +90°)—rather than measure *clockwise* (i.e., from the north direction x-axis, or 0°, towards the east direction y-axis, or +90°), as done in the [horizontal coordinate system](/wiki/Horizontal_coordinate_system "Horizontal coordinate system").[[2]](#cite_note-4) *(See graphic re "mathematics convention".)*

The spherical coordinate system of the *physics convention* can be seen as a generalization of the [polar coordinate system](/wiki/Polar_coordinate_system "Polar coordinate system") in [three-dimensional space](/wiki/Three-dimensional_space "Three-dimensional space").
It can be further extended to higher-dimensional spaces, and is then referred to as a [*hyperspherical coordinate system*](/wiki/N-sphere#Spherical_coordinates "N-sphere").

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Definition")]

To define a spherical coordinate system, one must designate an *origin* point in space, *O*, and two orthogonal directions: the *zenith reference* direction and the *azimuth reference* direction. These choices determine a reference plane that is typically defined as containing the point of origin and the *x– and y–axes*, either of which may be designated as the *azimuth reference* direction. The reference plane is perpendicular (orthogonal) to the zenith direction, and typically is designated "horizontal" to the zenith direction's "vertical". The spherical coordinates of a point P then are defined as follows:

* The *radius* or *radial distance* is the [Euclidean distance](/wiki/Euclidean_distance "Euclidean distance") from the origin *O* to *P*.
* The *inclination* (or *polar angle*) is the signed angle from the zenith reference direction to the line segment OP. (*Elevation* may be used as the polar angle instead of *inclination*; see below.)
* The *[azimuth](/wiki/Azimuth "Azimuth")* (or *azimuthal angle*) is the signed angle measured from the *azimuth reference* direction to the orthogonal projection of the radial line segment OP on the reference plane.

The sign of the azimuth is determined by designating the rotation that is the *positive* sense of turning about the zenith. This choice is arbitrary, and is part of the coordinate system definition.
(If the inclination is either zero or 180 degrees (= π radians), the azimuth is arbitrary. If the radius is zero, both azimuth and inclination are arbitrary.)

The [*elevation*](/wiki/Horizontal_coordinate_system "Horizontal coordinate system") is the signed angle from the x-y reference plane to the radial line segment OP, where positive angles are designated as upward, towards the zenith reference. *Elevation* is 90 degrees (= ⁠π/2⁠ radians) *minus inclination*. Thus, if the inclination is 60 degrees (= ⁠π/3⁠ radians), then the elevation is 30 degrees (= ⁠π/6⁠ radians).

In [linear algebra](/wiki/Linear_algebra "Linear algebra"), the [vector](/wiki/Euclidean_vector "Euclidean vector") from the origin O to the point P is often called the *[position vector](/wiki/Position_vector "Position vector")* of *P*.

### Conventions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Conventions")]

Several different conventions exist for representing spherical coordinates and prescribing the naming order of their symbols. The 3-tuple number set ![{\displaystyle (r,\theta ,\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d0fbf2e667b8c94c169165fc44e0d73e66c901a9) denotes radial distance, the polar angle—"inclination", or as the alternative, "elevation"—and the azimuthal angle. It is the common practice within the physics convention, as specified by [ISO](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") standard [80000-2:2019](/wiki/ISO_80000-2 "ISO 80000-2"), and earlier in [ISO 31-11](/wiki/ISO_31-11 "ISO 31-11") (1992).

*As stated above, this article describes the ISO "physics convention"—unless otherwise noted.*

However, some authors (including mathematicians) use the symbol *ρ* (rho) for radius, or radial distance, *φ* for inclination (or elevation) and *θ* for azimuth—while others keep the use of *r* for the radius; all which "provides a logical extension of the usual polar coordinates notation".[[3]](#cite_note-http://mathworld.wolfram.com/SphericalCoordinates.html-5) As to order, some authors list the azimuth *before* the inclination (or the elevation) angle. Some combinations of these choices result in a [left-handed](/wiki/Right-hand_rule "Right-hand rule") coordinate system. The standard "physics convention" 3-tuple set ![{\displaystyle (r,\theta ,\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d0fbf2e667b8c94c169165fc44e0d73e66c901a9) conflicts with the usual notation for two-dimensional [polar coordinates](/wiki/Polar_coordinate_system "Polar coordinate system") and three-dimensional [cylindrical coordinates](/wiki/Cylindrical_coordinate_system "Cylindrical coordinate system"), where θ is often used for the azimuth.[[3]](#cite_note-http://mathworld.wolfram.com/SphericalCoordinates.html-5)

Angles are typically measured in [degrees](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)") (°) or in [radians](/wiki/Radian "Radian") (rad), where 360° = 2π rad. The use of degrees is most common in geography, astronomy, and engineering, where radians are commonly used in mathematics and [theoretical physics](/wiki/Theoretical_physics "Theoretical physics"). The unit for radial distance is usually determined by the context, as occurs in applications of the 'unit sphere', see [applications](#Applications).

When the system is used to designate physical three-space, it is customary to assign positive to azimuth angles measured in the *counterclockwise* sense from the reference direction on the reference plane—as seen from the "zenith" side of the plane. This convention is used in particular for geographical coordinates, where the "zenith" direction is [north](/wiki/North "North") and the positive azimuth (longitude) angles are measured eastwards from some [prime meridian](/wiki/Prime_meridian "Prime meridian").

Major conventions

| coordinates set order | corresponding local geographical directions   (*Z*, *X*, *Y*) | right/left-handed |
| --- | --- | --- |
| (*r*, *θ*inc, *φ*az,right) | (*U*, *S*, *E*) | right |
| (*r*, *φ*az,right, *θ*el) | (*U*, *E*, *N*) | right |
| (*r*, *θ*el, *φ*az,right) | (*U*, *N*, *E*) | left |

**Note:** [Easting (E), Northing (N)](/wiki/Easting_and_northing "Easting and northing"), Upwardness (U). In the case of (*U*, *S*, *E*) the local [azimuth](/wiki/Azimuth "Azimuth") angle would be measured [counterclockwise](/wiki/Counterclockwise "Counterclockwise") from S to E.

### Unique coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Unique coordinates")]

Any spherical coordinate triplet (or tuple) ![{\displaystyle (r,\theta ,\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d0fbf2e667b8c94c169165fc44e0d73e66c901a9) specifies a single point of three-dimensional space. On the reverse view, any single point has infinitely many equivalent spherical coordinates. That is, the user can add or subtract any number of full turns to the angular measures without changing the angles themselves, and therefore without changing the point. It is convenient in many contexts to use negative radial distances, the convention being ![{\displaystyle (-r,\theta ,\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c4303c15d79785d06c7524a3122212e0b3ae84cf), which is equivalent to ![{\displaystyle (r,\theta {+}180^{\circ },\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cab7e4cba37608e0142734802ddf411f8b8ed421) or ![{\displaystyle (r,90^{\circ }{-}\theta ,\varphi {+}180^{\circ })}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d2618130ceec28d3e8fec406305a5f6f6ca2c32a) for any r, θ, and φ. Moreover, ![{\displaystyle (r,-\theta ,\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/beb79911beca5b2e82ba695b1a0b28fa6234141c) is equivalent to ![{\displaystyle (r,\theta ,\varphi {+}180^{\circ })}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f7047b347ebddd28dc07756b28171bca656dedf7).

When necessary to define a unique set of spherical coordinates for each point, the user must restrict the [range, aka interval](/wiki/Interval_(mathematics) "Interval (mathematics)"), of each coordinate. A common choice is:

* radial distance: *r* ≥ 0,
* polar angle: 0° ≤ *θ* ≤ 180°, or 0 rad ≤ *θ* ≤ π rad,
* azimuth : 0° ≤ *φ* < 360°, or 0 rad ≤ *φ* < 2π rad.

But instead of the interval [0°, 360°), the azimuth φ is typically restricted to the [half-open interval](/wiki/Interval_(mathematics)#Definitions "Interval (mathematics)") (−180°, +180°], or (−π, +π ] radians, which is the standard convention for geographic longitude.

For the polar angle θ, the range (interval) for inclination is [0°, 180°], which is equivalent to elevation range (interval) [−90°, +90°]. In geography, the latitude is the elevation.

Even with these restrictions, if the polar angle (inclination) is 0° or 180°—elevation is −90° or +90°—then the azimuth angle is arbitrary; and if r is zero, both azimuth and polar angles are arbitrary. To define the coordinates as unique, the user can assert the convention that (in these cases) the arbitrary coordinates are set to zero.

### Plotting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Plotting")]

To plot any dot from its spherical coordinates (*r*, *θ*, *φ*), where θ is inclination, the user would: move r units from the origin in the zenith reference direction (z-axis); then rotate by the amount of the azimuth angle (φ) about the origin *from* the designated *azimuth reference* direction, (i.e., either the x- or y-axis, see [Definition](#Definition), above); and then rotate *from* the z-axis by the amount of the θ angle.

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Applications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Spherical_coordinate_system.svg/330px-Spherical_coordinate_system.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Spherical_coordinate_system.svg)

In the ***mathematics convention***: A globe showing a [unit sphere](/wiki/Unit_sphere "Unit sphere"), with [tuple](/wiki/Tuple "Tuple") coordinates of point P (red): its radial distance r (red, not labeled); its azimuthal angle θ (not labeled); and its polar angle of *inclination* φ (not labeled). The radial distance upward along the [zenith–axis](/wiki/Zenith "Zenith") from the point of origin to the surface of the sphere is assigned the value unity, or 1. + In this image, r appears to equal 4/6, or .67, (of unity); i.e., four of the six 'nested shells' to the surface. The azimuth angle θ appears to equal positive 90°, as rotated *counterclockwise* from the azimuth-reference x–axis; and the inclination φ appears to equal 30°, as rotated from the zenith–axis. (Note the 'full' rotation, or inclination, from the zenith–axis to the y–axis is 90°).

Just as the two-dimensional [Cartesian coordinate system](/wiki/Cartesian_coordinate_system "Cartesian coordinate system") is useful—has a wide set of applications—on a planar surface, a two-dimensional spherical coordinate system is useful on the surface of a sphere. For example, one sphere that is described in *Cartesian coordinates* with the equation *x*2 + *y*2 + *z*2 = *c*2 can be described in *spherical coordinates* by the simple equation *r* = *c*. (In this system—*shown here in the mathematics convention*—the sphere is adapted as a [unit sphere](/wiki/Unit_sphere "Unit sphere"), where the radius is set to unity and then can generally be ignored, see graphic.)

This (unit sphere) simplification is also useful when dealing with objects such as [rotational matrices](/wiki/Rotation_matrix "Rotation matrix"). Spherical coordinates are also useful in analyzing systems that have some degree of symmetry about a point, including: [volume integrals](/wiki/Multiple_integral "Multiple integral") inside a sphere; the potential energy field surrounding a concentrated mass or charge; or global weather simulation in a planet's atmosphere.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Bosch_36W_column_loudspeaker_polar_pattern.png/330px-Bosch_36W_column_loudspeaker_polar_pattern.png)](/wiki/File:Bosch_36W_column_loudspeaker_polar_pattern.png)

The output pattern of the industrial [loudspeaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") shown here uses spherical polar plots taken at six frequencies

Three dimensional modeling of [loudspeaker](/wiki/Loudspeaker "Loudspeaker") output patterns can be used to predict their performance. A number of polar plots are required, taken at a wide selection of frequencies, as the pattern changes greatly with frequency. Polar plots help to show that many loudspeakers tend toward omnidirectionality at lower frequencies.

An important application of spherical coordinates provides for the [separation of variables](/wiki/Separation_of_variables "Separation of variables") in two [partial differential equations](/wiki/Partial_differential_equations "Partial differential equations")—the [Laplace](/wiki/Laplace_equation "Laplace equation") and the [Helmholtz equations](/wiki/Helmholtz_equation "Helmholtz equation")—that arise in many physical problems. The angular portions of the solutions to such equations take the form of [spherical harmonics](/wiki/Spherical_harmonics "Spherical harmonics"). Another application is [ergonomic design](/wiki/Ergonomic_design "Ergonomic design"), where r is the arm length of a stationary person and the angles describe the direction of the arm as it reaches out. The spherical coordinate system is also commonly used in 3D [game development](/wiki/Game_development "Game development") to rotate the camera around the player's position[[4]](#cite_note-6)

### In geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: In geography")]

Main article: [Geographic coordinate system](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system")

See also: [ECEF](/wiki/ECEF "ECEF")

Instead of inclination, the [geographic coordinate system](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") uses elevation angle (or *[latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude")*), in the range (aka [domain](/wiki/Interval_(mathematics) "Interval (mathematics)")) −90° ≤ *φ* ≤ 90° and rotated north from the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") plane. Latitude (i.e., *the angle* of latitude) may be either *[geocentric latitude](/wiki/Geocentric_latitude "Geocentric latitude")*, measured (rotated) from the Earth's center—and designated variously by *ψ*, *q*, *φ*′, *φ*c, *φ*g—or *[geodetic latitude](/wiki/Geodetic_latitude "Geodetic latitude")*, measured (rotated) from the observer's [local vertical](/wiki/Local_vertical "Local vertical"), and typically designated φ.
The polar angle (inclination), which is 90° minus the latitude and ranges from 0 to 180°, is called *[colatitude](/wiki/Colatitude "Colatitude")* in geography.

The azimuth angle (or *[longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude")*) of a given position on Earth, commonly denoted by λ, is measured in degrees east or west from some conventional reference [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") (most commonly the [IERS Reference Meridian](/wiki/IERS_Reference_Meridian "IERS Reference Meridian")); thus its domain (or range) is −180° ≤ *λ* ≤ 180° and a given reading is typically designated "East" or "West". For positions on the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") or other solid [celestial body](/wiki/Celestial_body "Celestial body"), the reference plane is usually taken to be the plane perpendicular to the [axis of rotation](/wiki/Axis_of_rotation "Axis of rotation").

Instead of the radial distance r geographers commonly use *[altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude")* above or below some local reference surface (*[vertical datum](/wiki/Vertical_datum "Vertical datum")*), which, for example, may be the [mean sea level](/wiki/Mean_sea_level "Mean sea level"). When needed, the radial distance can be computed from the altitude by adding the [radius of Earth](/wiki/Radius_of_Earth "Radius of Earth"), which is approximately 6,360 ± 11 km (3,952 ± 7 miles).

However, modern geographical coordinate systems are quite complex, and the positions implied by these simple formulae may be inaccurate by several kilometers. The precise standard meanings of *latitude, longitude* and *altitude* are currently defined by the [World Geodetic System](/wiki/World_Geodetic_System "World Geodetic System") (WGS), and take into account the flattening of the Earth at the poles (about 21 km or 13 miles) and many other details.

[Planetary coordinate systems](/wiki/Planetary_coordinate_system "Planetary coordinate system") use formulations analogous to the geographic coordinate system.

### In astronomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: In astronomy")]

A series of [astronomical coordinate systems](/wiki/Astronomical_coordinate_systems "Astronomical coordinate systems") are used to measure the elevation angle from several [fundamental planes](/wiki/Fundamental_plane_(spherical_coordinates) "Fundamental plane (spherical coordinates)"). These reference planes include:
the observer's [horizon](/wiki/Horizontal_coordinate_system "Horizontal coordinate system"), the [galactic equator](/wiki/Galactic_coordinate_system "Galactic coordinate system") (defined by the rotation of the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way")), the [celestial equator](/wiki/Celestial_equator "Celestial equator") (defined by [Earth's rotation](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation")), the plane of the [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic_coordinate_system "Ecliptic coordinate system") (defined by Earth's orbit around the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun")), and the plane of the earth [terminator](/wiki/Terminator_(solar) "Terminator (solar)") (normal to the instantaneous direction to the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun")).

## Coordinate system conversions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Coordinate system conversions")]

See also: [List of common coordinate transformations § To spherical coordinates](/wiki/List_of_common_coordinate_transformations#To_spherical_coordinates "List of common coordinate transformations")

As the spherical coordinate system is only one of many three-dimensional coordinate systems, there exist equations for converting coordinates between the spherical coordinate system and others.

### Cartesian coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Cartesian coordinates")]

The spherical coordinates of a point in the ISO convention (i.e. for physics: radius r, inclination θ, azimuth φ) can be obtained from its [Cartesian coordinates](/wiki/Cartesian_coordinate_system "Cartesian coordinate system") (*x*, *y*, *z*) by the formulae

![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}r&={\sqrt {x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}}\\\theta &=\arccos {\frac {z}{\sqrt {x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}}}=\arccos {\frac {z}{r}}={\begin{cases}\arctan {\frac {\sqrt {x^{2}+y^{2}}}{z}}&{\text{if }}z>0\\\pi +\arctan {\frac {\sqrt {x^{2}+y^{2}}}{z}}&{\text{if }}z<0\\+{\frac {\pi }{2}}&{\text{if }}z=0{\text{ and }}{\sqrt {x^{2}+y^{2}}}\neq 0\\{\text{undefined}}&{\text{if }}x=y=z=0\\\end{cases}}\\\varphi &=\operatorname {sgn} (y)\arccos {\frac {x}{\sqrt {x^{2}+y^{2}}}}={\begin{cases}\arctan({\frac {y}{x}})&{\text{if }}x>0,\\\arctan({\frac {y}{x}})+\pi &{\text{if }}x<0{\text{ and }}y\geq 0,\\\arctan({\frac {y}{x}})-\pi &{\text{if }}x<0{\text{ and }}y<0,\\+{\frac {\pi }{2}}&{\text{if }}x=0{\text{ and }}y>0,\\-{\frac {\pi }{2}}&{\text{if }}x=0{\text{ and }}y<0,\\{\text{undefined}}&{\text{if }}x=0{\text{ and }}y=0.\end{cases}}\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/754c539f15bfcc4e7448733ab8e31667a02b22ae)
where sgn(0) = 1.
The [inverse tangent](/wiki/Inverse_tangent "Inverse tangent") denoted in *φ* = arctan ⁠*y*/*x*⁠ must be suitably defined, taking into account the correct quadrant of (*x*, *y*), as done in the equations above. See the article on [atan2](/wiki/Atan2 "Atan2").

Alternatively, the conversion can be considered as two sequential [rectangular to polar conversions](/wiki/Polar_coordinate_system#Converting_between_polar_and_Cartesian_coordinates "Polar coordinate system"): the first in the Cartesian xy plane from (*x*, *y*) to (*R*, *φ*), where R is the projection of r onto the xy-plane, and the second in the Cartesian zR-plane from (*z*, *R*) to (*r*, *θ*). The correct quadrants for φ and θ are implied by the correctness of the planar rectangular to polar conversions.

These formulae assume that the two systems have the same origin, that the spherical reference plane is the Cartesian xy plane, that θ is inclination from the z direction, and that the azimuth angles are measured from the Cartesian x axis (so that the y axis has *φ* = +90°). If *θ* measures elevation from the reference plane instead of inclination from the zenith the arccos above becomes an arcsin, and the cos *θ* and sin *θ* below become switched.

Conversely, the Cartesian coordinates may be retrieved from the spherical coordinates (*radius* r, *inclination* θ, *azimuth* φ), where *r* ∈ [0, ∞), *θ* ∈ [0, π], *φ* ∈ [0, 2π), by
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}x&=r\sin \theta \,\cos \varphi ,\\y&=r\sin \theta \,\sin \varphi ,\\z&=r\cos \theta .\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/077ab5a9774e05d20497a377bd846f7da9ae95bb)

### Cylindrical coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Cylindrical coordinates")]

Main article: [Cylindrical coordinate system](/wiki/Cylindrical_coordinate_system "Cylindrical coordinate system")

[Cylindrical coordinates](/wiki/Cylindrical_coordinate_system "Cylindrical coordinate system") (*axial* *radius* *ρ*, *azimuth* *φ*, *elevation* *z*) may be converted into spherical coordinates (*central radius* *r*, *inclination* *θ*, *azimuth* *φ*), by the formulas

![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}r&={\sqrt {\rho ^{2}+z^{2}}},\\\theta &=\arctan {\frac {\rho }{z}}=\arccos {\frac {z}{\sqrt {\rho ^{2}+z^{2}}}},\\\varphi &=\varphi .\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/864addcc034827cf2a6448172fc66c10c31bf944)

Conversely, the spherical coordinates may be converted into cylindrical coordinates by the formulae

![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\rho &=r\sin \theta ,\\\varphi &=\varphi ,\\z&=r\cos \theta .\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3ee8633ef65e3e0d9ca8070f36cfba5ef231d1a0)

These formulae assume that the two systems have the same origin and same reference plane, measure the azimuth angle φ in the same senses from the same axis, and that the spherical angle θ is inclination from the cylindrical z axis.

## Ellipsoidal coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ellipsoidal coordinates")]

See also: [Ellipsoidal coordinates](/wiki/Ellipsoidal_coordinates "Ellipsoidal coordinates")

It is also possible to deal with ellipsoids in Cartesian coordinates by using a modified version of the spherical coordinates.

Let P be an ellipsoid specified by the level set

![{\displaystyle ax^{2}+by^{2}+cz^{2}=d.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/827cb48eb0365b2d3fd9836f2240073b7bbd1425)

The modified spherical coordinates of a point in P in the ISO convention (i.e. for physics: *radius* r, *inclination* θ, *azimuth* φ) can be obtained from its [Cartesian coordinates](/wiki/Cartesian_coordinate_system "Cartesian coordinate system") (*x*, *y*, *z*) by the formulae

![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}x&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {a}}}r\sin \theta \,\cos \varphi ,\\y&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {b}}}r\sin \theta \,\sin \varphi ,\\z&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {c}}}r\cos \theta ,\\r^{2}&=ax^{2}+by^{2}+cz^{2}.\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d944a89d434cfc696c3ef19c213eca989079ab10)

An infinitesimal volume element is given by

![{\displaystyle \mathrm {d} V=\left|{\frac {\partial (x,y,z)}{\partial (r,\theta ,\varphi )}}\right|\,dr\,d\theta \,d\varphi ={\frac {1}{\sqrt {abc}}}r^{2}\sin \theta \,\mathrm {d} r\,\mathrm {d} \theta \,\mathrm {d} \varphi ={\frac {1}{\sqrt {abc}}}r^{2}\,\mathrm {d} r\,\mathrm {d} \Omega .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ec24746775bfab4e0a5c94c2d7f7423342ea2c20)

The square-root factor comes from the property of the [determinant](/wiki/Determinant "Determinant") that allows a constant to be pulled out from a column:

![{\displaystyle {\begin{vmatrix}ka&b&c\\kd&e&f\\kg&h&i\end{vmatrix}}=k{\begin{vmatrix}a&b&c\\d&e&f\\g&h&i\end{vmatrix}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/924353c1c45455707700cfd0b784365f7e0e9b0d)

## Integration and differentiation in spherical coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Integration and differentiation in spherical coordinates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Kugelkoord-lokb-e.svg/250px-Kugelkoord-lokb-e.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kugelkoord-lokb-e.svg)

Unit vectors in spherical coordinates

The following equations (Iyanaga 1977) assume that the colatitude θ is the inclination from the positive z axis, as in the *physics convention* discussed.

The [line element](/wiki/Line_element "Line element") for an infinitesimal displacement from (*r*, *θ*, *φ*) to (*r* + d*r*, *θ* + d*θ*, *φ* + d*φ*) is
![{\displaystyle \mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} =\mathrm {d} r\,{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}+r\,\mathrm {d} \theta \,{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}+r\sin {\theta }\,\mathrm {d} \varphi \,\mathbf {\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}} ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b19bc4d661d9cf7cd09027568387e46ee452953f)
where
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}&=\sin \theta \cos \varphi \,{\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+\sin \theta \sin \varphi \,{\hat {\mathbf {y} }}+\cos \theta \,{\hat {\mathbf {z} }},\\{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}&=\cos \theta \cos \varphi \,{\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+\cos \theta \sin \varphi \,{\hat {\mathbf {y} }}-\sin \theta \,{\hat {\mathbf {z} }},\\{\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}}&=-\sin \varphi \,{\hat {\mathbf {x} }}+\cos \varphi \,{\hat {\mathbf {y} }}\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/15c06e5b94e6981470001062b634252d936bbaa3)
are the local orthogonal [unit vectors](/wiki/Unit_vectors "Unit vectors") in the directions of increasing r, θ, and φ, respectively,
and **x̂**, **ŷ**, and **ẑ** are the unit vectors in Cartesian coordinates. The linear transformation to this right-handed coordinate triplet is a [rotation matrix](/wiki/Rotation_matrix "Rotation matrix"),
![{\displaystyle R={\begin{pmatrix}\sin \theta \cos \varphi &\sin \theta \sin \varphi &{\hphantom {-}}\cos \theta \\\cos \theta \cos \varphi &\cos \theta \sin \varphi &-\sin \theta \\-\sin \varphi &\cos \varphi &{\hphantom {-}}0\end{pmatrix}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/572737b6c535b62bebbff974417fceeb4f7bf035)

This gives the transformation from the Cartesian to the spherical, the other way around is given by its inverse.
Note: the matrix is an [orthogonal matrix](/wiki/Orthogonal_matrix "Orthogonal matrix"), that is, its inverse is simply its [transpose](/wiki/Transpose "Transpose").

The Cartesian unit vectors are thus related to the spherical unit vectors by:
![{\displaystyle {\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf {\hat {x}} \\\mathbf {\hat {y}} \\\mathbf {\hat {z}} \end{bmatrix}}={\begin{bmatrix}\sin \theta \cos \varphi &\cos \theta \cos \varphi &-\sin \varphi \\\sin \theta \sin \varphi &\cos \theta \sin \varphi &{\hphantom {-}}\cos \varphi \\\cos \theta &-\sin \theta &{\hphantom {-}}0\end{bmatrix}}{\begin{bmatrix}{\boldsymbol {\hat {r}}}\\{\boldsymbol {\hat {\theta }}}\\{\boldsymbol {\hat {\varphi }}}\end{bmatrix}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/abdfd135b8aef9bd75094a0ea040705611ee14ea)

The general form of the formula to prove the differential line element, is[[5]](#cite_note-q74503-7)
![{\displaystyle \mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} =\sum _{i}{\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial x_{i}}}\,\mathrm {d} x_{i}=\sum _{i}\left|{\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial x_{i}}}\right|{\frac {\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial x_{i}}}{\left|{\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial x_{i}}}\right|}}\,\mathrm {d} x_{i}=\sum _{i}\left|{\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial x_{i}}}\right|\,\mathrm {d} x_{i}\,{\hat {\boldsymbol {x}}}_{i},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1e6cecacf81c2b593936c0ff6afe6943d5d44bc8)
that is, the change in ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {r} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/eca0f46511c4c986c48b254073732c0bd98ae0c1) is decomposed into individual changes corresponding to changes in the individual coordinates.

To apply this to the present case, one needs to calculate how ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {r} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/eca0f46511c4c986c48b254073732c0bd98ae0c1) changes with each of the coordinates. In the conventions used,
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {r} ={\begin{bmatrix}r\sin \theta \,\cos \varphi \\r\sin \theta \,\sin \varphi \\r\cos \theta \end{bmatrix}},x_{1}=r,x_{2}=\theta ,x_{3}=\varphi .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/556087c5124e9c701cf908bd97c4c0f79bdebfc7)

Thus,
![{\displaystyle {\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial r}}={\begin{bmatrix}\sin \theta \,\cos \varphi \\\sin \theta \,\sin \varphi \\\cos \theta \end{bmatrix}}=\mathbf {\hat {r}} ,\quad {\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial \theta }}={\begin{bmatrix}r\cos \theta \,\cos \varphi \\r\cos \theta \,\sin \varphi \\-r\sin \theta \end{bmatrix}}=r\,{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}},\quad {\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial \varphi }}={\begin{bmatrix}-r\sin \theta \,\sin \varphi \\{\hphantom {-}}r\sin \theta \,\cos \varphi \\0\end{bmatrix}}=r\sin \theta \,\mathbf {\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}} .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2f27d52dcdefa273fb893ae95e21f41075c45a56)

The desired coefficients are the magnitudes of these vectors:[[5]](#cite_note-q74503-7)
![{\displaystyle \left|{\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial r}}\right|=1,\quad \left|{\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial \theta }}\right|=r,\quad \left|{\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial \varphi }}\right|=r\sin \theta .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5c0a7b35118ec0b06b2a3072dab425862af68f85)

The [surface element](/wiki/Surface_integral "Surface integral") spanning from θ to *θ* + d*θ* and φ to *φ* + d*φ* on a spherical surface at (constant) radius r is then
![{\displaystyle \mathrm {d} S_{r}=\left\|{\frac {\partial {\mathbf {r} }}{\partial \theta }}\times {\frac {\partial {\mathbf {r} }}{\partial \varphi }}\right\|\mathrm {d} \theta \,\mathrm {d} \varphi =\left|r{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}\times r\sin \theta {\boldsymbol {\hat {\varphi }}}\right|\mathrm {d} \theta \,\mathrm {d} \varphi =r^{2}\sin \theta \,\mathrm {d} \theta \,\mathrm {d} \varphi ~.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d9bace6ed91e600d026364bf6adb830d39575980)

Thus the differential [solid angle](/wiki/Solid_angle "Solid angle") is
![{\displaystyle \mathrm {d} \Omega ={\frac {\mathrm {d} S_{r}}{r^{2}}}=\sin \theta \,\mathrm {d} \theta \,\mathrm {d} \varphi .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ef27ca6dea913eae67956940f173d690bcaa71f8)

The surface element in a surface of polar angle θ constant (a cone with vertex at the origin) is
![{\displaystyle \mathrm {d} S_{\theta }=r\sin \theta \,\mathrm {d} \varphi \,\mathrm {d} r.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/83704aaf946f20a832c3f4b83e8a6dad0abcc313)

The surface element in a surface of azimuth φ constant (a vertical half-plane) is
![{\displaystyle \mathrm {d} S_{\varphi }=r\,\mathrm {d} r\,\mathrm {d} \theta .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b917e1558b487b2650c9546b77dd8bd3911dc878)

The [volume element](/wiki/Volume_element "Volume element") spanning from r to *r* + d*r*, θ to *θ* + d*θ*, and φ to *φ* + d*φ* is specified by the [determinant](/wiki/Determinant "Determinant") of the [Jacobian matrix](/wiki/Jacobian_matrix "Jacobian matrix") of [partial derivatives](/wiki/Partial_derivative "Partial derivative"),
![{\displaystyle J={\frac {\partial (x,y,z)}{\partial (r,\theta ,\varphi )}}={\begin{pmatrix}\sin \theta \cos \varphi &r\cos \theta \cos \varphi &-r\sin \theta \sin \varphi \\\sin \theta \sin \varphi &r\cos \theta \sin \varphi &{\hphantom {-}}r\sin \theta \cos \varphi \\\cos \theta &-r\sin \theta &{\hphantom {-}}0\end{pmatrix}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/244a10c8233858491f03e21326900c5ef2a24fd4)
namely
![{\displaystyle \mathrm {d} V=\left|{\frac {\partial (x,y,z)}{\partial (r,\theta ,\varphi )}}\right|\,\mathrm {d} r\,\mathrm {d} \theta \,\mathrm {d} \varphi =r^{2}\sin \theta \,\mathrm {d} r\,\mathrm {d} \theta \,\mathrm {d} \varphi =r^{2}\,\mathrm {d} r\,\mathrm {d} \Omega ~.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/858b36ce1577f1133ecd96c49b15b7a22fd44c29)

Thus, for example, a function *f*(*r*, *θ*, *φ*) can be integrated over every point in **R**3 by the [triple integral](/wiki/Multiple_integral#Spherical_coordinates "Multiple integral")
![{\displaystyle \int \limits _{0}^{2\pi }\int \limits _{0}^{\pi }\int \limits _{0}^{\infty }f(r,\theta ,\varphi )r^{2}\sin \theta \,\mathrm {d} r\,\mathrm {d} \theta \,\mathrm {d} \varphi ~.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fc3c4d6cc5aea6c3307480cbd378e8a52b4c771c)

The [del](/wiki/Del "Del") operator in this system leads to the following expressions for the [gradient](/wiki/Gradient "Gradient") and [Laplacian](/wiki/Laplacian "Laplacian") for scalar fields,
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\nabla f&={\partial f \over \partial r}{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}+{1 \over r}{\partial f \over \partial \theta }{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}+{1 \over r\sin \theta }{\partial f \over \partial \varphi }{\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}},\\[8pt]\nabla ^{2}f&={1 \over r^{2}}{\partial  \over \partial r}\left(r^{2}{\partial f \over \partial r}\right)+{1 \over r^{2}\sin \theta }{\partial  \over \partial \theta }\left(\sin \theta {\partial f \over \partial \theta }\right)+{1 \over r^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta }{\partial ^{2}f \over \partial \varphi ^{2}}\\[8pt]&=\left({\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial r^{2}}}+{\frac {2}{r}}{\frac {\partial }{\partial r}}\right)f+{1 \over r^{2}\sin \theta }{\partial  \over \partial \theta }\left(\sin \theta {\frac {\partial }{\partial \theta }}\right)f+{\frac {1}{r^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta }}{\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial \varphi ^{2}}}f~,\\[8pt]\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c085b5d702755a656ad961a224d007f50c7dfe76)And it leads to the following expressions for the [divergence](/wiki/Divergence "Divergence") and [curl](/wiki/Curl_(mathematics) "Curl (mathematics)") of [vector fields](/wiki/Vector_field "Vector field"),

![{\displaystyle \nabla \cdot \mathbf {A} ={\frac {1}{r^{2}}}{\partial  \over \partial r}\left(r^{2}A_{r}\right)+{\frac {1}{r\sin \theta }}{\partial  \over \partial \theta }\left(\sin \theta A_{\theta }\right)+{\frac {1}{r\sin \theta }}{\partial A_{\varphi } \over \partial \varphi },}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a37ea03511d9354ab7d87c6343ca1988cc9f6e21)![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\nabla \times \mathbf {A} ={}&{\frac {1}{r\sin \theta }}\left[{\partial  \over \partial \theta }\left(A_{\varphi }\sin \theta \right)-{\partial A_{\theta } \over \partial \varphi }\right]{\hat {\mathbf {r} }}\\[4pt]&{}+{\frac {1}{r}}\left[{1 \over \sin \theta }{\partial A_{r} \over \partial \varphi }-{\partial  \over \partial r}\left(rA_{\varphi }\right)\right]{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}\\[4pt]&{}+{\frac {1}{r}}\left[{\partial  \over \partial r}\left(rA_{\theta }\right)-{\partial A_{r} \over \partial \theta }\right]{\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}},\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2c2477820afa14dd84fdaba95b49d463fba1ee65)

Further, the inverse Jacobian in Cartesian coordinates is
![{\displaystyle J^{-1}={\begin{pmatrix}{\dfrac {x}{r}}&{\dfrac {y}{r}}&{\dfrac {z}{r}}\\\\{\dfrac {xz}{r^{2}{\sqrt {x^{2}+y^{2}}}}}&{\dfrac {yz}{r^{2}{\sqrt {x^{2}+y^{2}}}}}&{\dfrac {-\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)}{r^{2}{\sqrt {x^{2}+y^{2}}}}}\\\\{\dfrac {-y}{x^{2}+y^{2}}}&{\dfrac {x}{x^{2}+y^{2}}}&0\end{pmatrix}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b1ee27e9b9d1bde64fc1eb122123c9393cc4aaa6)
The [metric tensor](/wiki/Metric_tensor "Metric tensor") in the spherical coordinate system is ![{\displaystyle g=J^{T}J}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/28fe097305f712f3d16d198eb3c6b954e85e0f21).

## Distance and angle in spherical coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Distance and angle in spherical coordinates")]

In spherical coordinates, given two points with φ being the azimuthal coordinate
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\mathbf {r} }&=(r,\theta ,\varphi ),\\{\mathbf {r} '}&=(r',\theta ',\varphi ')\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/831476c0fe4f8efcadf7c8f8d15854692c095f78)
The distance between the two points can be expressed as[[6]](#cite_note-8)
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\mathbf {D} }&={\sqrt {r^{2}+r'^{2}-2rr'(\sin {\theta }\sin {\theta '}\cos {(\varphi -\varphi ')}+\cos {\theta }\cos {\theta '})}}\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/23b7e4cc6e166b841e381400eed1b2a6532c229c)

The angle ![{\displaystyle \gamma }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a223c880b0ce3da8f64ee33c4f0010beee400b1a) between the two points can be found from their dot product in Cartesian coordinates:
![{\displaystyle \cos \gamma =\cos \theta \cos \theta '+\sin \theta \sin \theta '(\cos \phi \cos \phi '+\sin \phi \sin \phi ')}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6b2422ef89298bc15c0bbb67f3ba752ecf4016ba)
which by the [angle difference identity](/wiki/Angle_difference_identity "Angle difference identity") for cosine is[[7]](#cite_note-9)
![{\displaystyle \cos \gamma =\cos \theta \cos \theta '+\sin \theta \sin \theta '\cos(\phi -\phi ')}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7b55f2fee3ab3ac4d03f32104cd4587ff702eb48)

## Kinematics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Kinematics")]

In spherical coordinates, the position of a point or particle (although better written as a [triple](/wiki/Tuple "Tuple") ![{\displaystyle (r,\theta ,\varphi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d0fbf2e667b8c94c169165fc44e0d73e66c901a9)) can be written as[[8]](#cite_note-Cameron2019-10)
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {r} =r\mathbf {\hat {r}} .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/110a6b3fd53a0d13978bb353cfe0b9beec94b05b)
Its velocity is then[[8]](#cite_note-Cameron2019-10)
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {v} ={\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} }{\mathrm {d} t}}={\dot {r}}\mathbf {\hat {r}} +r\,{\dot {\theta }}\,{\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}+r\,{\dot {\varphi }}\sin \theta \,\mathbf {\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8d85f6040e0860104f5ea2a0e36d69c16645a082)
and its acceleration is[[8]](#cite_note-Cameron2019-10)
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\mathbf {a} ={}&{\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v} }{\mathrm {d} t}}\\[1ex]={}&{\hphantom {+}}\;\left({\ddot {r}}-r\,{\dot {\theta }}^{2}-r\,{\dot {\varphi }}^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta \right)\mathbf {\hat {r}} \\&{}+\left(r\,{\ddot {\theta }}+2{\dot {r}}\,{\dot {\theta }}-r\,{\dot {\varphi }}^{2}\sin \theta \cos \theta \right){\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}\\&{}+\left(r{\ddot {\varphi }}\,\sin \theta +2{\dot {r}}\,{\dot {\varphi }}\,\sin \theta +2r\,{\dot {\theta }}\,{\dot {\varphi }}\,\cos \theta \right){\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}}\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cb548d3f8b0f22d189e8bca1bd0c6e4815908bb0)

The  [angular momentum](/wiki/Angular_momentum#Orbital_angular_momentum_in_three_dimensions "Angular momentum") is
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {L} =\mathbf {r} \times \mathbf {p} =\mathbf {r} \times m\mathbf {v} =mr^{2}\left(-{\dot {\varphi }}\sin \theta \,\mathbf {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}} +{\dot {\theta }}\,{\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/894627af2ef006b4661d3c69124952aaad33c4a0)
Where ![{\displaystyle m}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0a07d98bb302f3856cbabc47b2b9016692e3f7bc) is mass. In the case of a constant φ or else *θ* = ⁠π/2⁠, this reduces to [vector calculus in polar coordinates](/wiki/Polar_coordinate_system#Vector_calculus "Polar coordinate system").

The corresponding  [angular momentum operator](/wiki/Angular_momentum_operator#Orbital_angular_momentum_in_spherical_coordinates "Angular momentum operator") then follows from the phase-space reformulation of the above,
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {L} =-i\hbar ~\mathbf {r} \times \nabla =i\hbar \left({\frac {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}{\sin(\theta )}}{\frac {\partial }{\partial \phi }}-{\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi }}}{\frac {\partial }{\partial \theta }}\right).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d0b80b74515f5d8e2d1add4775567b619ffab4ac)

The torque is given as[[8]](#cite_note-Cameron2019-10)
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {\tau } ={\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {L} }{\mathrm {d} t}}=\mathbf {r} \times \mathbf {F} =-m\left(2r{\dot {r}}{\dot {\varphi }}\sin \theta +r^{2}{\ddot {\varphi }}\sin {\theta }+2r^{2}{\dot {\theta }}{\dot {\varphi }}\cos {\theta }\right){\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta }}}+m\left(r^{2}{\ddot {\theta }}+2r{\dot {r}}{\dot {\theta }}-r^{2}{\dot {\varphi }}^{2}\sin \theta \cos \theta \right){\hat {\boldsymbol {\varphi }}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f1563bc0c76c4e5abd2871be7b11267e3edce8b4)

The [kinetic energy](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy") is given as[[8]](#cite_note-Cameron2019-10)
![{\displaystyle E_{k}={\frac {1}{2}}m\left[\left({\dot {r}}\right)^{2}+\left(r{\dot {\theta }}\right)^{2}+\left(r{\dot {\varphi }}\sin \theta \right)^{2}\right]}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/635f4dc13b75d1e1b4fe939530265e5408b97921)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spherical_coordinate_system&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** An [oriented line](/wiki/Oriented_line "Oriented line"), so the polar angle is an [oriented angle](/wiki/Oriented_angle "Oriented angle") reckoned from the polar axis main [direction](/wiki/Direction_(geometry) "Direction (geometry)"), not its opposite direction.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** If the polar axis is made to coincide with positive *z*-axis, the azimuthal angle *φ* may be calculated as the angle between either of the *x*-axis or *y*-axis and the [orthogonal projection](/wiki/Orthogonal_projection "Orthogonal projection") of the radial line onto the reference *x-y*-plane — which is [orthogonal](/wiki/Orthogonality "Orthogonality") to the *z*-axis and passes through the fixed point of origin, completing a three-dimensional [Cartesian coordinate system](/wiki/Cartesian_coordinate_system "Cartesian coordinate system").
